title: Calorietekort Calculator
description: Bereken uw ideale calorietekort voor veilig gewichtsverlies.

faq.title: Veelgestelde Vragen
faq.q1: Hoe wordt de waarde van 7.700 kcal per kilogram afgeleid?
faq.a1: Een kilogram vetweefsel bevat ongeveer 7.700 kcal energie. Dus een cumulatief calorietekort van 7.700 kcal zou theoretisch 1 kg vet moeten produceren. In de praktijk omvat gewichtsverlies ook waterverlies en spiermassa.
faq.q2: Wat is het verschil tussen calorietekort en caloriebeperking?
faq.a2: Calorietekort is het verschil tussen ingenomen en verbrande calorieën in een bepaalde periode. Caloriebeperking verwijst naar de totale calorie-inname ten opzichte van onderhoudsniveaus.
faq.q3: Hoe snel kan ik veilig gewicht verliezen?
faq.a3: Veilig gewichtsverlies is over het algemeen 0,5–1 kg per week voor de meeste volwassenen. Dit vereist een tekort van 500–1.000 kcal/dag. Snellere verliezen verhogen het risico op verlies van spiermassa en tekorten aan voedingsstoffen.
faq.q4: Wat gebeurt er als ik te weinig eet voor te lang?
faq.a4: Langdurige ernstige caloriebeperking kan metabolische aanpassing veroorzaken — het lichaam verlaagt BMR met 15–25% om energie te besparen. Dit maakt gewichtsverlies progressief moeilijker.
faq.q5: Moet ik pauzes nemen van mijn calorietekort?
faq.a5: Dieetpauzes van 1–2 weken op onderhoudsniveau kunnen metabolische aanpassing helpen voorkomen, naleving op lange termijn verbeteren en hormoonspiegels herstellen.
faq.q6: Hoe bereken ik mijn TDEE voor het instellen van het tekort?
faq.a6: TDEE = BMR × activiteitsfactor. Trek het gewenste tekort (300–500 kcal) af van TDEE voor geleidelijk gewichtsverlies.
faq.q7: Moet lichaamsbeweging worden opgenomen in de tekortsberekening?
faq.a7: TDEE omvat al fysieke activiteit via de activiteitsfactor. Velen berekenen liever BMR × 1,2 (sedentair) en voegen apart verbrande calorieën toe van lichaamsbeweging.
faq.q8: Hoe pas ik het calorietekort aan als mijn gewicht verandert?
faq.a8: Herbereken TDEE elke 5–10 kg gewichtsverlies. Naarmate u gewicht verliest, daalt uw BMR en worden de verbrande calorieën per activiteit minder.

content.whatIs:
A calorie deficit calculator determines the precise daily calorie intake needed to achieve your weight loss goal at a chosen rate. It starts from your TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure — the calories your body burns each day at your current activity level) and calculates the reduction needed to create enough of an energy deficit to lose body fat at the rate you select.

A calorie deficit occurs when you consume fewer calories than your body expends. When the body cannot find all the energy it needs from food, it turns to stored energy — primarily body fat — to make up the difference. One kilogram of body fat contains approximately 7,700 kcal of stored energy. A sustained daily deficit of 550 kcal therefore depletes approximately 0.5 kg of body fat per week. This energy balance principle, grounded in thermodynamics, is the foundation of every evidence-based weight loss approach regardless of the specific diet framework used.

This calculator uses the 7,700 kcal/kg equation endorsed by nutritional science and recommended by the CDC, NIH, and WHO for sustainable weight loss planning. It provides your daily calorie target, the total deficit needed to reach your goal weight, and a realistic timeline — giving you a complete, science-based plan rather than a generic target.
content.formula:
Daily Deficit = (weekly loss goal × 7,700) ÷ 7

Where 7,700 kcal ≈ energy stored in 1 kg of body fat

Daily Calorie Target = TDEE − Daily Deficit
Total Deficit Needed = (current weight − target weight) × 7,700
Weeks to Goal = (current weight − target weight) ÷ weekly loss goal

Safe Range Check:
  Minimum daily intake: 1,200 kcal (women) / 1,500 kcal (men)
  Maximum recommended weekly loss: 1.0 kg/week (for non-obese individuals)
content.example:
Example 1 — Moderate deficit, woman:
Current: 75 kg | Target: 65 kg | TDEE: 2,100 kcal | Goal: 0.5 kg/week
Daily deficit = (0.5 × 7,700) ÷ 7 = 550 kcal
Daily target: 2,100 − 550 = 1,550 kcal
Total deficit needed: 10 × 7,700 = 77,000 kcal
Timeline: 10 ÷ 0.5 = 20 weeks (~5 months)

Example 2 — Mild deficit, man:
Current: 90 kg | Target: 82 kg | TDEE: 2,800 kcal | Goal: 0.25 kg/week
Daily deficit = (0.25 × 7,700) ÷ 7 = 275 kcal
Daily target: 2,800 − 275 = 2,525 kcal
Timeline: 8 ÷ 0.25 = 32 weeks — but very comfortable and muscle-preserving

Example 3 — Aggressive deficit (max recommended):
Current: 95 kg | Target: 80 kg | TDEE: 2,900 kcal | Goal: 1.0 kg/week
Daily deficit = (1.0 × 7,700) ÷ 7 = 1,100 kcal
Daily target: 2,900 − 1,100 = 1,800 kcal
Timeline: 15 weeks — but requires high protein and resistance training to preserve muscle
content.howItWorks_1: Enter your current weight, target weight, and your TDEE (use our TDEE Calculator if you do not know it). Then select your desired weekly weight loss rate from mild (0.25 kg/week) to aggressive (1.0 kg/week). The calculator uses these inputs to determine the necessary daily calorie deficit.
content.howItWorks_2: Click Calculate. The formula divides the energy equivalent of your weekly loss goal by 7 to get the daily deficit, then subtracts it from your TDEE to produce your daily calorie target. It also calculates the total deficit needed and your estimated timeline.
content.howItWorks_3: Use your daily calorie target as the anchor for your meal planning. Track your food intake with any calorie tracking app. Weigh yourself weekly (same day, same time, fasted) and compare to the expected loss rate. If actual loss differs significantly from expected, recalculate TDEE based on real-world data.
content.whyItMatters:
The single biggest reason diets fail is not willpower — it is the absence of a rational, personalized calorie target. Generic diet plans prescribe arbitrary calorie levels that may be wildly inappropriate for a specific individual's metabolism, activity level, and body size. A 1,200 kcal plan appropriate for a 50 kg sedentary woman is dangerously restrictive for a 90 kg moderately active man whose TDEE is 2,800 kcal. Without calculating the right number for you specifically, weight loss becomes guesswork.

Calorie deficit science also explains why crash diets fail long-term. An extreme deficit (1,000+ kcal/day below TDEE) produces rapid initial weight loss but triggers metabolic adaptation — the body reduces BMR by 15–30%, increases hunger hormones (ghrelin rises, leptin falls), and decreases NEAT (unconscious movement). The result is that the effective deficit shrinks to near zero within weeks, weight loss stalls, and the elevated hunger makes the diet unsustainable. The research on this is unambiguous: moderate, consistent deficits of 300–600 kcal/day produce better long-term outcomes than aggressive restriction.

Understanding your specific deficit also allows you to plan with realistic timelines. Losing 15 kg takes approximately 30 weeks at a 500 kcal/day deficit — not 6 weeks, as many fad diets claim. Setting expectations based on real numbers prevents the frustration and abandonment that unrealistic timelines cause. Knowing your timeline lets you commit to a plan that actually works.
content.limitations:
The 7,700 kcal/kg equation assumes all weight lost comes from pure fat tissue. In reality, weight loss always includes some water loss, glycogen depletion, and — especially at aggressive deficits — lean mass (muscle and organ tissue). In the early weeks of a calorie deficit, significant water and glycogen loss often causes rapid scale weight reduction (2–5 kg in the first 1–2 weeks) that is not fat. Conversely, water retention from stress, sodium, menstrual cycle, and inflammation can mask real fat loss for 1–3 weeks.

The formula also cannot account for individual metabolic adaptation. Two people following identical deficits from the same starting TDEE can lose weight at meaningfully different rates due to genetic differences in metabolic efficiency, differences in actual NEAT response to restriction, and variation in food thermodynamics. This is why the calculated timeline is a projection, not a guarantee — reality will differ by ±20–30% for most individuals.

This calculator provides a calorie target only — it does not specify macronutrient distribution, meal timing, food quality, or other factors that substantially influence the composition of weight lost (fat vs muscle), dietary adherence, and health outcomes during restriction. Protein intake in particular is critical: at the same calorie deficit, high protein intake (1.6–2.4 g/kg) preserves significantly more lean mass than low protein intake.
content.tips_1: Choose a deficit rate you can sustain for the full duration. A 0.5 kg/week deficit maintained for 20 weeks produces better results than a 1.0 kg/week deficit abandoned after 6 weeks due to hunger and fatigue. Adherence over time is the most powerful predictor of fat loss success — more powerful than the specific deficit size.
content.tips_2: Prioritize protein at 1.6–2.2 g/kg body weight per day during your deficit. High protein intake is the single most powerful tool for preserving muscle mass during calorie restriction, increasing satiety (protein is the most filling macronutrient per calorie), and boosting metabolic rate through the thermic effect. If you hit your protein target within your calorie budget, fill remaining calories with carbohydrates and fat in proportions that suit your preferences.
content.tips_3: Include resistance training 2–4 times per week during your deficit. Strength training sends a strong signal to the body to preserve (or even build) muscle tissue even while in a calorie deficit. Without this signal, a portion of your weight loss will inevitably come from muscle, reducing metabolic rate and changing body composition unfavorably. You do not need to lift heavy — bodyweight training is sufficient to provide the muscle-preserving signal.
content.tips_4: Recalculate your TDEE and calorie target every 4–6 weeks as your weight changes. A 10 kg reduction in body weight typically reduces TDEE by 150–250 kcal/day. Failing to adjust your target downward as you lose weight is the most common reason fat loss plateaus — your original deficit gradually disappears as your smaller body requires fewer calories at the same intake level.
